summary: I recall being with my father at Blue Ridge, North Carolina, which was a YMCA training center where meetings and classes were held in the summer. It was there that my father expressed his indignation about lynchings in the South, especially one that had occurred in Georgia. Blue Ridge was a place where these discussions about law and justice took place, shaping my understanding of racial violence and legal lapses in the South.

I remember a lynching that happened in Georgia which was widely reported in newspapers and magazines. My father showed me a picture of a small, meek sheriff holding the rope used in the hanging, symbolizing the failure of law enforcement in the South. This event deeply disturbed us and was part of the broader context of racial violence we discussed during my youth.

In the 1930s, the Federal Council of Churches issued fact bulletins on each lynching that occurred, inviting local reporters to provide detailed accounts without editorializing. They asked me to investigate two lynchings on the Eastern Shore of Maryland around 1935, and I reported my findings to them. Although there was some controversy and they considered distancing themselves from my report, they eventually published the bulletin, highlighting the Council's role in documenting racial violence.

The Eastern Shore of Maryland is a culturally distinct enclave separated from the western part of the state by Chesapeake Bay. Around 1935, two lynchings occurred there—one in Snow Hill and another in Salisbury—that deeply disturbed me. The region had an inferiority complex expressed through overdefensiveness, and many locals reacted with pride to the lynchings, even resisting outside intervention from the Western Shore.

Snow Hill was the site of one of the lynchings on the Eastern Shore of Maryland around 1935. A black man was taken out and lynched there, though I don't recall all the details. This event was part of a troubling pattern of racial violence in the area that I tried to document and bring attention to.

Salisbury, a larger town on Maryland's Eastern Shore, was the site of a brutal lynching around 1935 involving an eighteen-year-old black boy who had a conflict with his employer. Despite attempts by a World War I veteran and a courthouse employee to prevent the lynching, the mob succeeded in hanging and burning the boy's body. I went there to investigate and reported the facts to the Federal Council of Churches, which caused a significant local and statewide reaction.

A courageous veteran of the First World War played a notable role during the Salisbury lynching by trying to block the mob with his car on a narrow bridge and speaking out against their actions. His bravery stood out to me as a rare act of resistance in the face of overwhelming violence. This event highlighted the complex social dynamics and the failure of authorities to protect innocent lives during that era.

After the Salisbury lynching, I went to see the Maryland Attorney General to urge prosecution of the mob members, especially after a list of participants was published openly. Although I couldn't meet the Attorney General himself, his assistant explained that the matter was under local jurisdiction and not their responsibility. This response was frustrating and reflected the challenges of seeking justice for racial violence in Maryland at the time.

In Baltimore, a small but active group of black communists, along with some white allies including Albert Blumberg from Johns Hopkins University, organized a delegation to appeal to the Governor of Maryland for action against lynching. I joined them in visiting the Governor's office, where we faced resistance and ultimately no response. Baltimore was a center of political activism and interracial cooperation in the fight against racial injustice during this period.

Johns Hopkins University was my academic home, where Joseph S. Ames was the president during the time of the lynching controversies. The university was known for its commitment to academic freedom, and Ames was sympathetic to my efforts, though he responded diplomatically to protests from the Eastern Shore. Albert Blumberg, a young faculty member, also joined in activism against lynching, showing that Hopkins had individuals engaged in social justice issues.

Albert Blumberg was a young faculty member at Johns Hopkins University who joined black communists and me in a delegation to the Governor of Maryland to demand action against lynching. He was one of the white allies actively involved in civil rights efforts in Baltimore during the 1930s. His participation symbolized the interracial cooperation that was necessary to confront racial violence at the time.

When we visited the Governor of Maryland's office to demand action after the lynchings on the Eastern Shore, he was heavily guarded and reluctant to meet with our full delegation. Only a few of us, including Albert Blumberg and myself, were allowed in, and when I pressed him, I even suggested impeachment for failing to protect citizens. Unfortunately, the Governor took no action, reflecting the political unwillingness to confront racial violence at the time.

H. L. Mencken was a well-known critic who, despite his usual sardonic style, was serious and forceful in condemning the lynchings and the authorities' neglect. After my report on the Salisbury lynching was published, Mencken invited me to talk late at night and expressed strong sympathy for my efforts. He also publicly criticized the university president's cautious response, showing his commitment to exposing social injustices.

Joseph S. Ames was the President of Johns Hopkins University during the lynching controversies in the 1930s. I had a good personal relationship with him, and he was supportive of academic freedom and my work, though his official responses to protests from the Eastern Shore were somewhat apologetic and diplomatic. Mencken famously mocked Ames's cautious stance, but our relations remained positive throughout.

Isaiah Bowman was mentioned in the transcript but not discussed in detail, so I have no personal story or direct interaction to share about him. He was likely known to me through academic or professional circles, but the transcript does not provide further context or events involving him.

Occidental College was referenced in the transcript but not elaborated upon in my narrative. I do not recall specific experiences or events related to Occidental College in the context of the lynching investigations or my work in Maryland and Baltimore.

I approached the Maryland State Police after the lynchings to ask if they could have taken stronger measures, such as placing a machine gun on the jail roof, to prevent the mob violence. This reflected my frustration with the inadequate protection offered by law enforcement during these tragic events. The State Police's response, however, was not detailed in the transcript, but their role was clearly insufficient in stopping the lynchings.

ROTC was mentioned briefly but not in connection with the lynching events or my investigations. There is no detailed account or personal involvement with ROTC in the transcript, so I cannot provide a story or context related to it.
